author: No author specified
content: Conceptual Architecture Framework

author: No author specified
content: 1. Purpose​

author: No author specified
content: This text is a first draft. Further work is defered until it is clear for what (actual) purposes it will be used, so that we can make sure it is fit for such purposes.

author: No author specified
content: According to ISO 42010, the core of any architecture description (of a "System of Interest") consists of a specification of (identified) stakeholders and their concerns, and a set of '(architecture) views' (that are governed by '(architecture) viewpoints') that address these concerns. A high quality architecture description will be complete in its list of stakeholders (or stakeholder roles) and their concerns. Also, it will have addressed each of them in one or more of its views.

author: No author specified
content: In an SSI context, there typically is no single "System of Interest". Instead there are many such systems, run by different parties, that work together - i.e. exchange data (typically: (verifiable) credentials). In such contexts, individual parties can and will perform a variety of functions in various roles, the most prominent of which are issuer, holder, and validator1.

author: No author specified
content: The purpose of this document is

author: No author specified
content: to provide a (conceptual) framework that shows how individual "Systems of Interest" that various parties run can be conceived to work together, i.e. exchange data (typically: (verifiable) credentials) between them;

author: No author specified
No author specified
content: design/define time: governance (and governance processes);

author: No author specified
content: operational/runtime: kinds of actions that (designated) employees of the parties will need to be capable of performing on their behalf;

author: No author specified
content: the creation and maintenance of the various policies for an individual capability, so that such employees will know how to operate; that is: when they actually execute such actions, they will do so in accordance with the intention of the party on whose behalf they do so.

author: No author specified
content: Thus, this document can be used as a mental model that can help create and maintain coherence and consistence between governance architectures and technical architectures.

author: No author specified
content: This document assumes the reader is familiar with the eSSIF-Lab mental models, in particular the one about Parties, Actors and Actions.

author: No author specified
content: 2. Context​

author: No author specified
content: SSI is all about the exchange of data, typically in the form of (verifiable) credentials (data that represents a set of claims, each of which is a statement about a particular entity (the subject of the claim)).

author: No author specified
content: The context within which SSI is used consists of various parties, each of which has set its own objectives (its mission being the most prominent one). They have processes that seek to achieve the realization of these objectives. We only focus on the information processes (production processes are out of scope), i.e. processes in which various kinds of data are manipluated. In order to exchange data, information processes need a number of capabilities that enable them to:

author: No author specified
content: formulate requests (to other parties) for sending data, and receiving responses and obtaining such data that can easily be validated, i.e.: that it an agent that receives/obtains such data can easily determine whether or not it is valid (in the eyes of its principal) to be used for further processing and the purposes that such processing serves. We provisionally call  this the 'usage capability'.

author: No author specified
content: offer data that it has to other parties, i.e. create and publish advertisements for such offerings, and means by which such other parties can request for such data and obtain it (all, of course, compliant with the policies that this party has specified). We provisionally call this the 'provisioning capability';

author: No author specified
content: store data, for later use. We provisionally call this the 'storage capability'.

author: No author specified
content: Parties may have any or all of these capabilities, as illustrated in the following figure. The figure also shows that (implementations of) such capabilities must have the ability to communicate with (implementations of) capabilities of other parties.

author: No author specified
content: Figure 1. Context - Information processes and Capabilities.

The figure also shows that (implementations of) such capabilities must have the ability to communicate with (implementations of) capabilities of other parties.  Figure 1. Context - Information processes and Capabilities. Disclaimer: the figure is meant to introduce the idea of capabilities that support information processes. It only shows the most prominent ones that are needed for SSI. 3. Capabilities​

author: No author specified
content: Disclaimer: the figure is meant to introduce the idea of capabilities that support information processes. It only shows the most prominent ones that are needed for SSI.

author: No author specified
content: 3. Capabilities​

author: No author specified
content: A party is said to have a capability if it can (operationally, i.e. at runtime) have particular things done according to the policies that specify the party-specific details of how that should be done, constraints that should be adhered to, etc. This implies that the party has (digital and/or human) employees that have been assigned tasks to do the associated work, and/or outsource such tasks.

author: No author specified
content: The following subsections provide a draft description of the capabilities mentioned in the figure above.

author: No author specified
content: 3.1. Usage Capability​

author: No author specified
content: The purpose of the usage capability is to ensure that whenever a party intends to process data for some purpose, it can:

author: No author specified
content: find parties that can provide such data;

author: No author specified
content: formulate criteria (that are specific for each purpose) to distinguish between data that is (not) valid for the purpose(s) that the data will be processed for;

author: No author specified
content: provide the circumstances and/or satisfy the conditions for its (designated) employees so that they can request such data, obtain and verify data from responses to such requests, to validate the data for the purpose(s) the data was requested, and to process the (validated) data in the appropriate information processes.

author: No author specified
content: This capability consists of

author: No author specified
content: runtime/operational parts, where agents request, verify, and validate such data (the further processing is done using capabilities of the information processes);

author: No author specified
content: a design-time part, in which the party determines for every information process when data is needed, what kinds of data that would be, what the verification criteria are, what the validation criteria are, and how this data is to be subsequently processed.

author: No author specified
content: a mapping part, in which such criteria are converted into policies for employees (that are tasked to do the operational work) can read and understand, and use to perform the verification, validation and other, related kinds of actions in the way as intended by the party on whose behalf they will be acting.

author: No author specified
content: 3.2. Storage Capability​

author: No author specified
content: The purpose of the storage capability is to ensure that a party can cache data (typically: credentials) in such a way that the confidentiality (privacy) and integrity of the stored data is guaranteed, and that only designated employees (either of that party itself, but also of other parties) are provided with rights and/or duties to access (read, write) that cache.

author: No author specified
content: This capability consists of

author: No author specified
content:
runtime/operational parts, such as

a wallet (the actual store/cache), that is capable of servicing requests for storing, retrieving, modifying and/or deleting credentials (perhaps also other data) from the store/cache, according to the access control policy of its owner;
(at least) one holder (i.e. an employee that is capable of communicating with other employees for the purpose of

requesting and obtaining credentials that it subsequently stores), and
receiving presentation requests, processing such requests, and responding with either a presentation or an error.2

author: No author specified
content: a wallet (the actual store/cache), that is capable of servicing requests for storing, retrieving, modifying and/or deleting credentials (perhaps also other data) from the store/cache, according to the access control policy of its owner;

author: No author specified
content:
(at least) one holder (i.e. an employee that is capable of communicating with other employees for the purpose of

requesting and obtaining credentials that it subsequently stores), and
receiving presentation requests, processing such requests, and responding with either a presentation or an error.2

author: No author specified
content: requesting and obtaining credentials that it subsequently stores), and

author: No author specified
content: receiving presentation requests, processing such requests, and responding with either a presentation or an error.2

author: No author specified
content: a design-time part, in which the party manages the mandates (including consents) for its own employees, as well as for employees of other parties.

author: No author specified
content: 3.3. Provisioning Capability​

author: No author specified
content: The purpose of the provisioning capability is to ensure that a party can

author: No author specified
content: create and subsequently issue credential of specific kinds upon request, and

author: No author specified
content: advertise the kinds of credentials that it is capable and willing to issue, in such a way that other parties can find these advertisements and use their contents to determine whether or not such credentials contain data that are valid for being processed by (or on behalf of) tht party for some specific purpose.

author: No author specified
content: A party that has this capability will have 'employees' that

author: No author specified
No author specified
content: supplying credentials upon request;

author: No author specified
content: revoking credentials whenever (parts of) their contents is no longer considered to be true in the knowledge of the party;

author: No author specified
content: signing credentials on behalf of the party;

author: No author specified
content: presenting the party's offerings for the kinds of credentials that it is willing and able to issue (such that other parties can decide whether or not the contents of such credentials are valid for one or more of its purposes);

author: No author specified
content: etc.

author: No author specified
No author specified
content: constructs the advertisements for the kinds of credentials it will be issuing;

author: No author specified
content: establishes the criteria that the components that it has running (and act on its behalf), must use to determine whether or not to service requests that they receive;

author: No author specified
content: decides what related services to use (such as services for revocation3), and

author: No author specified
content:
a mapping part, in which

the service request criteria are converted into policies that employees (that are tasked to do the operational work on the party's behalf) can read and understand, and use to decide whether or not to service issuing requests in the way as intended by the party on whose behalf they act.
additional (provisioning-related) services are
it is determined at what endpoint(s) requests for supplying such credentials will be serviced;

author: No author specified
content: the service request criteria are converted into policies that employees (that are tasked to do the operational work on the party's behalf) can read and understand, and use to decide whether or not to service issuing requests in the way as intended by the party on whose behalf they act.

author: No author specified
content: additional (provisioning-related) services are

author: No author specified
content: it is determined at what endpoint(s) requests for supplying such credentials will be serviced;

author: No author specified
content: Provisioning also includes the issuing of credentials that contain claims that mandate4 specific agents (the claim's subjects) for specific kinds of access (read, update, write, ...) to functions that are provided by designated agents of the party with that provisioning capability.

author: No author specified
content: 4.4. Revocation Capability​

author: No author specified
content: The purpose of the revocation capability is

author: No author specified
content: to ensure that that credentials that have been issued on behalf of the party that runs this component, are revoked whenever a situation is detected (in some information process) that shows that one or more of the claims in the credential are no longer true (in the knowledge of that party)

author: No author specified
content: parties that intend to use (data from) a credential that has been issued by the party that has the revocation capability, are enabled to learn whether or not that (data from) that credential has been revoked.

author: No author specified
content: This capability consists of

author: No author specified
No author specified
content: for signalling credentials that need to be revoked;

author: No author specified
content: that implement one or more revocation mechanisms, that can be used to revoke such credentials.

author: No author specified
content: a design-time part, in which the party maintains criteria for signalling credentials to be revoked, and determines which kind(s) of revocation mechanisms should be used for which kind(s) of credentials.

author: No author specified
content: a mapping part, in which the party maintains the set of policies that employees (that are tasked to do the operational work on the party's behalf) can read and understand, and use to decide whether or not to revoke credentials, and do the actual revocation thereof.

author: No author specified
content: 4. Functional Components​

author: No author specified
content: While parties typically have all these capabilities, they each will have their own, subjective expectations regarding the particular ways in which such capabilities should operate. Such expectations must be made explicit, e.g. by means of policies that employees that are mandated to perform associated actions can read and interpret such that they execute these actions according to the intentions of the party on whose behalf they do so.

author: No author specified
content: The following figure shows how parties can furnish the aforementioned capabilities in terms of some generic functional components.

author: No author specified
content: Figure 1. Context - Information processes and Capabilities.

author: No author specified
imgMeta: While parties typically have all these capabilities, they each will have their own, subjective expectations regarding the particular ways in which such capabilities should operate. Such expectations must be made explicit, e.g. by means of policies that employees that are mandated to perform associated actions can read and interpret such that they execute these actions according to the intentions of the party on whose behalf they do so. The following figure shows how parties can furnish the aforementioned capabilities in terms of some generic functional components.  Figure 1. Context - Information processes and Capabilities. You can see e.g. that a capability may be constructed by one or more functional components. The 'storage capability', for example, has components for exchanging credentials)

author: No author specified
content: You can see e.g. that a capability may be constructed by one or more functional components. The 'storage capability', for example, has

author: No author specified
content: a wallet component, that holds credentials (and perhaps other data), has a simple interface for doing CRUD operations (the basic operations on data stores), and a mechanism to ensure that requests will only be serviced if the requesting component presents a proper mandate (permission, consent, …) for the requested operation.

author: No author specified
content: a holder component, that can interact with issuer and validator components for exchanging credentials)

author: No author specified
content: Note that every component in the figure has two parts:

author: No author specified
content: the IT that is running on some computer. Note that this IT may be controlled by a party (typically called 'service provider') that is not the party on whose behalf the IT will be functioning.

author: No author specified
content: a policy, authored by the party on whose behalf the component functions, where the policy specifies the party-specific ways of its functioning. In the Wallet example, it would contain e.g., the access control criteria for deciding whether or not to service the various requests.

author: No author specified
content: What the figure does not show is that a mapping or translation activity may be called for to translate the 'business speak' that states the contents of the policies into 'employee-specific speak', i.e. data/texts that employees (that are tasked to execute the associated operational actions), can read and interpret such that when they use them to execute such actions on behalf of the party that authored the (business) policies, they will do so in accordance with the intensions of that party. Since employees can be human, non-human, and typically have different capabilities for understanding policy-data, a single business-policy might need to be translated into multiple operational policies.

author: No author specified
content: Footnotes​

author: No author specified
content: The SSI community typically talks about verification (and the associated role of verifier), and ignores validation. However, since data is exchanged so that the party that receives presentations can actually use that data for the specific purpose(s) that the presentations are requested. This not only means that such data must be verifiable (and verified), but particularly that it must also be validatable, and only used when it turns out to be valid for further processing. ↩

author: No author specified

However, since data is exchanged so that the party that receives presentations can actually use that data for the specific purpose(s) that the presentations are requested. This not only means that such data must be verifiable (and verified), but particularly that it must also be validatable, and only used when it turns out to be valid for further processing. ↩

author: No author specified
content: eSSIF-Lab Glossary

author: No author specified
content: Action​

author: No author specified
content: something that is actually done (a 'unit of work' that is executed) by a single actor (on behalf of a given party), as a single operation, in a specific context.

author: No author specified
content: Actor​

author: No author specified
content: entity that can act (do things/execute action), e.g. people, machines, but not organization.

author: No author specified
content: Advertisement​

author: No author specified
content: an offer by a specific party to provide a (type of) credential, where the offer includes (a reference to) the syntax and semantics specifications of that credential, and also lists various other characteristics that enable other parties to decide whether or not a credential that the advertising party has issued under this offer, is valid to be processed in one or more of its information processes.

author: No author specified
content: Agent​

author: No author specified
content: an actor that is executing an action on behalf of a party (called the principal of that actor).

author: No author specified
content: Assertion​

author: No author specified
content: a declaration/statement, made by a specific party, that something is the case.

author: No author specified
content: Assessment Framework​

author: No author specified
content: the combination of a set of criteria that an auditor can assess by applying them to an entity (of a specific kind), and the procedures that this auditor will follow for doing so, for the purpose of establishing the extent in which that entity conforms to a stated set of requirements.

author: No author specified
content: Attribute​

author: No author specified
content: data, that represents a characteristic that a party (the owner of the attribute) has attributed to an entity (which is the subject of that attribute).

author: No author specified
content: Authority (Centralized or Decentralized)​

author: No author specified
content: a party of which certain decisions, ideas, rules etc. are followed by other parties.

author: No author specified
content: Capability (of an Actor)​

author: No author specified
content: the potential of an actor to execute a (named) coherent set of action (a 'function', or 'task') on behalf of some party.

author: No author specified
content: Capability (of a Party)​

author: No author specified
content: the (named) combination of (a) the means of a specific party to get something done, (b) the party governance process that ensures that its (business) policies for getting that something done are being created and maintained, and (c) the party management process that creates and maintains the (operational) policies, such that every employee that has a task in getting this something done can find and interpret a policy and use it as it executes action in such tasks.

author: No author specified
content: Colleague​

author: No author specified
content: two or more (digital or non-digital) agent that have the same principal (i.e. party on whose behalf they are executing an action).

author: No author specified
content: Commitment Decision​

author: No author specified
content: the decision of that party whether or not to commit to that business transaction, i.e. (promise) to fulfill the obligation that the associated transaction agreement proposal would impose on that party once it were signed.

author: No author specified
content: Communication Channel​

author: No author specified
content: a (digital or non-digital) means by which two actor can exchange messages with one another.

author: No author specified
content: Communication Session​

author: No author specified
content: a time interval during which two actor have an established communication channel that does not exist outside of that time interval.

author: No author specified
content: Community​

author: No author specified
content: a party, consisting of at least two different parties (the members of the community) that seek to collaborate with each other so that each of them can achieve its individual objective more efficiently and/or effectively.

author: No author specified
content: Compliance level​

author: No author specified
content: a measure for stating how well an entity conforms with a stated (set of) requirement(s).

author: No author specified
content: Compliance management​

author: No author specified
content: the process that is run by (or on behalf of) a specific party for the purpose of managing its compliance objectives.

author: No author specified
content: Compliance Objective​

author: No author specified
content: an objective, owned by a party, that aims to reach and maintain a state of affairs in which a specific set of entities that this party controls are in accordance with a specific set of requirements (e.g., laws, regulations, standards, etc.).

author: No author specified
content: Compliance​

author: No author specified
content: the state of realization of a set of conformance criteria or normative framework of a party.

author: No author specified
content: Concept​

author: No author specified
content: the ideas/thoughts behind a classification of entities (what makes entities in that class 'the same').

author: No author specified
content: Control level​

author: No author specified
content: a measure for the efficiency and effectiveness in which a control produces the results specified by its control objective

author: No author specified
content: Control Objective​

author: No author specified
content: an objective, owned by a party, that aims to contribute to the realization of another (set of) objective of that party, by producing results that this party needs to realize these other objective.

author: No author specified
content: Control Process​

author: No author specified
content: a process, owned and run by a party, to ensure that a specific subset of its control objective are realized.

author: No author specified
content: Control​

author: No author specified
content: the combination of resources (e.g. people, tools, budgets, time) and processes that are tasked to realize a specific control objective of a particular party.

author: No author specified
content: Controller​

author: No author specified
content: the role that an actor performs as it is executing actions on that entity for the purpose of ensuring that the entity will act/behave, or be used, in a particular way.

author: No author specified
content: Controllership​

author: No author specified
content: the capability of an actor to execute action on a specific entity for the purpose of ensuring that this entity will act/behave, or be used, in a particular way.

author: No author specified
content: Corpus of Terminology​

author: No author specified
content: the documentation that describes the knowledge around a set of term and concept.

author: No author specified
content: Credential Catalogue​

author: No author specified
content: a functional component that has the capability to register and advertise the information about credential types that their respective governing parties have decided to disclose so as to enable other parties to decide whether or not it is beneficial for them to use credential of such types.

author: No author specified
content: Credential Type​

author: No author specified
content: the specification of the contents, properties, constraints etc. that credential of this type must have/comply with.

author: No author specified
content: Credential​

author: No author specified
content: data, representing a set of assertion (claims, statements), authored and signed by, or on behalf of, a specific party.

author: No author specified
content: Data​

author: No author specified
content: something (tangible) that can be used to communicate a meaning (which is intangible/information).

author: No author specified
content: Decentralized GRC Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that can be used to explain one's thinking about topics related to Governance, Risk management and Compliance (GRC) in a decentralized context, i.e., a context in which parties all autonomously do their own GRC.

author: No author specified
content: Decentralized Risk Management Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that can be used to explain how individual parties can efficiently and effectively perform their own risk management processes, while taking into account that there are other parties that have expectations towards them.

author: No author specified
content: Decision Making Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that can be used to explain how parties would, could, or should reason in order to reach good conclusions and make good decisions. This can be used as a basis for understanding the information needs of parties as they need to decide e.g. whether or not to commit to a Transaction proposal, or whether or not data is valid for some purpose. This pattern is based on Toulmin's use of arguments (of which a pragmatical text can be found here)

author: No author specified
content: Decision​

author: No author specified
content: the conclusion that a party inferences from a set of data that it considers to be facts.

author: No author specified
content: Define​

author: No author specified
content: to provide a criterion and a term, where the criterion can be used by people to determine whether or not something is an instance/example of a concept (or other semantic unit), and the term is used to refer to that concept, or an arbitrary instance thereof.

author: No author specified
content: Definition​

author: No author specified
content: a text that helps parties to have the same understanding about the meaning of (and concept behind) a term, ideally in such a way that these parties can determine whether or not they make the same distinction.

author: No author specified
content: Definition Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a mental model that describes the relations between a concept (or any other semantic unit), the term(s) that are used to refer to it, and the criteria to use for determining whether or not something is an instance (example) of that semantic unit.

author: No author specified
content: Delegate​

author: No author specified
content: the transferral of ownership of one or more obligation of a party (the delegator), including the associated accountability, to another party (the delegatee)), which implies that the delegatee can realize such obligation as it sees fit.

author: No author specified
content: Dependent​

author: No author specified
content: an entity for the caring for and/or protecting/guarding/defending of which a guardianship arrangement has been established.

author: No author specified
content: Dictionary​

author: No author specified
content: an alphabetically sorted list of term with various meanings they may have in different contexts.

author: No author specified
content: Documentation Interoperability​

author: No author specified
content: the property that a documentation system of making its content comprehensible for a variety of people that come from different backgrounds.

author: No author specified
content: Duties and Rights Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that can be used to explain what a generic duties and rights consists of (based on Hofeld's theories), and relates it to jurisdictions, parties and legal entities.

author: No author specified
content: Ecosystem​

author: No author specified
content: a set of at least two (autonomous) parties (its 'members') whose individual work complements that of other members, and is of benefit to the set as a whole.

author: No author specified
content: Employee​

author: No author specified
content: a (human or non-human (digital)) actor for whom/which it is realistic that it might execute action on behalf of a party (called the employer of that actor).

author: No author specified
content: Employer​

author: No author specified
content: a party on whose behalf a (human or non-human) actor (called an employee of that party) might execute action.

author: No author specified
content: Employment Contract​

author: No author specified
content: an agreement/contract between two parties, one of which controls a set of actor (the subject of the contract), that states (or refers to) all (sets of) rights and duties under which these actor can and/or must work for the other party.

author: No author specified
content: Entity​

author: No author specified
content: someone or something that is known to exist.

author: No author specified
content: Scope: essifLabTerminology​

author: No author specified
content: specification of the eSSIF-Lab scope.

author: No author specified
content: Expectation​

author: No author specified
content: an objective that is owned by a party for having a specific (set of) result(s) produced, where the actual production thereof is (going to be) outsourced to another party.

author: No author specified
content: Framework (Conceptual)​

author: No author specified
content: a set of assumptions, concepts, values, and practices that constitutes a way of viewing reality.

author: No author specified
content: Glossary​

author: No author specified
content: an alphabetically sorted list of term with the (single) meaning it has in (at least) one context.

author: No author specified
content: Governance and Management Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that can be used to explain how parties organize that their objectives are realized, either by doing the associated work themselves, or by arranging for other parties to do that.

author: No author specified
content: Governance​

author: No author specified
content: the act or process of governing or overseeing the realization of (the results associated with) a set of objective by the owner of these objective, in order to ensure they will be fit for the purposes that this owner intends to use them for.

author: No author specified
content: Governor​

author: No author specified
content: a role that a party (better: employee of a specific party) performs as it governs a (coherent) set of expectation of that party, within a particular focus.

author: No author specified
content: Guardian​

author: No author specified
content: a party that has been assigned rights and duties in a Guardianship Arrangement for the purpose of caring for and/or protecting/guarding/defending the entity that is the dependent in that Guardianship Arrangement.

author: No author specified
content: Guardianship Arrangement​

author: No author specified
content: guardianship Arrangement (in a Jurisdiction): the specification of a set of rights and duties between legal entities of the jurisdiction that enforces these rights and duties, for the purpose of caring for and/or protecting/guarding/defending one or more of these entities.

author: No author specified
content: Guardianship-type​

author: No author specified
content: a class of guardianship arrangements within the jurisdiction that governs and manages them.

author: No author specified
content: Guardianship (in a Jurisdiction)​

author: No author specified
content: the specification of a set of rights and duties between legal entities of the jurisdiction that enforces these rights and duties, for the purpose of caring for and/or protecting/guarding/defending one or more of these entities. (Synonym of Guardianship Arrangement)

author: No author specified
content: Guardianship Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that can be used to explain what a generic guardianship consists of, and how it relates to guardians, dependents, jurisdictions, etc.

author: No author specified
content: Holder Policy​

author: No author specified
content: a digital policy that enables an operational holder component to function in accordance with the objective of its principal.

author: No author specified
content: Holder​

author: No author specified
content: a component that implements the capability to handle presentation requests from a peer agent, produce the requested data (a presentation) according to its principal's holder-policy, and send that in response to the request.

author: No author specified
content: Human Being​

author: No author specified
content: a person of flesh and blood (homo sapiens), that we classify both as a party,an actor, and a jurisdiction.

author: No author specified
content: Identification Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that can be used to explain the mechanisms that parties may use to identify entities, and mechanisms for communicating with another parties such that both parties can identify an entity and know whether or not they identify the same entity.

author: No author specified
content: Identifier​

author: No author specified
content: a character string that is being used for the identification of some entity (yet may refer to 0, 1, or more entities, depending on the context within which it is being used).

author: No author specified
content: Identifier Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that can be used to explain how identifiers are defined and used for identification purposes.

author: No author specified
content: Identify​

author: No author specified
content: an act, by or on behalf of a party, that results in the selection of either

author: No author specified
content: a single partial identity that the party owns, given some (observed or received) data, or

author: No author specified
content: a single entity from a given set of entities that is the subject of a specified partial identity that the party owns.

author: No author specified
content: Identity​

author: No author specified
content: the combined knowledge about that entity of all parties, i.e. the union of all partial identities of which that entity is the subject.

author: No author specified
content: Identity Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that can be used to explain how digital identities work, how this relates to (attributes in) credentials, and how all this can be made to work in SSI contexts.

author: No author specified
content: Information Process​

author: No author specified
content: a coherent set of [actions@] that are (to be) performed by a single [party@], in which data (that is controlled by this party is created, read, updated and/or deleted.

author: No author specified
content: Isuer Policy​

author: No author specified
content: a digital policy that enables an operational issuer component to function in accordance with the objective of its principal.

author: No author specified
content: Issuer​

author: No author specified
content: a component that implements the capability to construct credential from data objects, according to the content of its principal's issuer-Policy (specifically regarding the way in which the credential is to be digitally signed), and pass it to the wallet-component of its principal allowing it to be issued.

author: No author specified
content: Jurisdiction​

author: No author specified
content: the composition of a legal system (legislation, enforcement thereof, and conflict resolution), a party that governs that legal system, a scope within which that legal system is operational, and one or more objective for the purpose of which the legal system is operated. See also the Jurisdictions pattern.

author: No author specified
content: Jurisdiction Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that can be used to explain what a generic jurisdiction consists of, and relates it to parties and legal entities.

author: No author specified
content: Knowledge​

author: No author specified
content: the (intangible) sum of what is known by a specific party, as well as the familiarity, awareness or understanding of someone or something by that party.

author: No author specified
content: Legal Entity​

author: No author specified
content: an entity that is known by, recognized to exist, and registered in that jurisdiction.

author: No author specified
content: Legal Jurisdiction​

author: No author specified
content: a jurisdiction that is governed/operated by a governmental body.

author: No author specified
content: Legal System​

author: No author specified
content: a system in which rules are defined, and mechanisms for their enforcement and conflict resolution are (implicitly or explicitly) specified.

author: No author specified
content: Management​

author: No author specified
content: the act or process of managing or actually realizing of (the results associated with) a set of objective by the owner of these objective.

author: No author specified
content: Mandate​

author: No author specified
content: a right or duty that one party (the mandator) has granted to another party or employee (the mandatee) for executing a specific (set of) action in the name of, and under responsibility/accountability of, this (first) party.

author: No author specified
content: Mandates, Delegation and Hiring Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that can be used to explain the ideas behind Mandating, Delegating, Hiring and their relations.

author: No author specified
content: Mental Model​

author: No author specified
content: a description, both casual and formal, of a set of concept (ideas), relations between them, and constraints, that together form a coherent and consistent 'viewpoint', or 'way of thinking' about a certain topic.

author: No author specified
content: Mental Model Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that can be used to explain how to create, maintain and use (decentralized) vocabularies (terminologies) that groups of people can use for the specific purposes they pursue.

author: No author specified
content: Mission​

author: No author specified
content: an objective of a party that provides an answer to the question why that party exists - i.e. its 'raison d'être'.

author: No author specified
content: Normative framework​

author: No author specified
content: a set of rules that are followed and/or criteria that remain fulfilled by (a specific kind of) entities whose behavior and/or properties are characterized as 'normal'.

author: No author specified
content: Objective​

author: No author specified
content: something toward which a party (its owner) directs effort (an aim, goal, or end of action).

author: No author specified
content: Obligation​

author: No author specified
content: an objective that is owned by the party for producing a specific (set of) result(s) that are to be used (consumed) by that party and/or other parties.

author: No author specified
content: Onboarding​

author: No author specified
content: a process that is run for a specific (set of) actor on behalf of a specific party, that terminates successfully if and  only if the party has (a) established the suitability of the actor for executing certain kinds of action on its behalf, (b) ensured that their mutual rights and duties are properly specified and will be appropriately enforced, and (c) provided the circumstances/contexts within which the actor is enabled to do so.

author: No author specified
content: Organization​

author: No author specified
content: a party that is capable of setting objective and making sure these are realized by actor that it has onboarded and/or by (vetted) parties that are committed to contribute to these objective.

author: No author specified
content: Outsourcing​

author: No author specified
content: the state of affairs in which a party has an objective (better: an expectation) for the realization of a (set of) result(s), where the actual production of these results is expected to be done by a party other than itself.

author: No author specified
content: Owned​

author: No author specified
content: an entity over which another entity (its owner) has the power (duty, right) to enjoy it, dispose of it and control it; that power is limited to (the scope of) that jurisdiction, and by its rules.

author: No author specified
content: Owner​

author: No author specified
content: the role that a party performs when it is exercising its legal, rightful or natural title to control that entity.

author: No author specified
content: Ownership​

author: No author specified
content: a relationship between two entities that exists within the scope of control of a jurisdiction, in which one of them (called the owner) has legal, rightful or natural rights and/or duties to enjoy, dispose of, and control the other (called the owned).

author: No author specified
content: Partial identity​

author: No author specified
content: all knowledge that a specific party (= the owner of the partial identity) has about that entity (= the 'subject' of the partial identity).

author: No author specified
content: Participant​

author: No author specified
content: a party is negotiating (or has negotiated) a transaction agreement.

author: No author specified
content: Party, Actor and Actions Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that can be used to explain how things get done. It answers questions such as: 'Who/what does things?', 'How are their actions being guided/controlled?', 'Who controls whom/what?', 'Who/what may be held accountable?'.

author: No author specified
content: Party Representation Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that can be used to explain how parties can be represented in various circumstances.

author: No author specified
content: Party​

author: No author specified
content: an entity that sets its objective, maintains its knowledge, and uses that knowledge to pursue its objective in an autonomous (sovereign) manner. Humans and organization are the typical examples.

author: No author specified
content: Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a description, both casual and formal, of a set of concept (ideas), relations between them, and constraints, that together form a coherent and consistent 'viewpoint', or 'way of thinking' about a certain topic.

author: No author specified
content: Peer Actor​

author: No author specified
content: the actor with whom/which this other actor is communicating in that communication session.

author: No author specified
content: Peer-agent​

author: No author specified
content: the agent with whom/which this other agent is communicating in that communication session.

author: No author specified
content: Peer Party​

author: No author specified
content: a party that also participates in that business transaction.

author: No author specified
content: Policy​

author: No author specified
content: a (set of) rules, working-instructions, preferences and other guidance for the execution of one or more kinds of action, that agent of the party that governs the policy have access to and can interpret such that this results in these action being executed as intended by that party.

author: No author specified
content: Presentation Request​

author: No author specified
content: a (signed) digital message that a verifier component sends to a holder component asking for specific data from one or more verifiable credential that are issued by specific Parties.

author: No author specified
content: Presentation​

author: No author specified
content: a (signed) digital message that a holder component may send to a verifier component that contains data derived from one or more verifiable credential (that (a colleague component of) the holder component has received from issuer components of one or more parties), as a response to a specific presentation request of a Verifier component.

author: No author specified
content: Principal​

author: No author specified
content: the party for whom, or on behalf of whom, the actor is executing an action (this actor is then called an agent of that party).

author: No author specified
content: Property (of a Concept)​

author: No author specified
content: a connection or association between a concept and a primitive data element, such as a text or a number, that represents some characteristic that instances of the concept may have.

author: No author specified
content: Qualified Data​

author: No author specified
content: data that comes with assurances, at least regarding its provenance and integrity (immutability), that make this data valid to be used for specific purposes of individual parties.

author: No author specified
content: Relation (between Concepts)​

author: No author specified
content: a (significant) connection or association between two or more concepts.

author: No author specified
content: Revocation component​

author: No author specified
content: a component that implements the capability to revoke credential that are issued by its principal, according to its principal revocation policy.

author: No author specified
content: Revocation Policy​

author: No author specified
content: a digital policy that enables an operational revocation component to function in accordance with the objective of its principal.

author: No author specified
content: Revoke/Revocation​

author: No author specified
content: the act, by or on behalf of the party that has issued the credential, of no longer vouching for the correctness or any other qualification of (arbitrary parts of) that credential.

author: No author specified
content: Risk level​

author: No author specified
content: a measure for the deviation of the intended realization (results) of a specific objective that its owner uses to represent the priority with which the risk of that objective should be reckoned with.

author: No author specified
content: Risk management​

author: No author specified
content: a process that is run by (or on behalf of) a specific party for the purpose of managing the risk that it owns (thereby realizing specific risk objectives).

author: No author specified
content: Risk Objective​

author: No author specified
content: an objective, owned by a party, that aims to reach and maintain a state of affairs in which the risk associated with a specific set of its objective become, and/or remain, acceptable.

author: No author specified
content: Risk Owner​

author: No author specified
content: the party that is the owner of the objective to which a risk is associated.

author: No author specified
content: Risk​

author: No author specified
content: the effects that uncertainty (i.e. a lack of information, understanding or knowledge of events, their consequences or likelihoods) can have on the intended realization of an objective of a party.

author: No author specified
content: Role name​

author: No author specified
content: name (text) that refers to (and identifies) a role in a specific context.

author: No author specified
content: Role​

author: No author specified
content: a defined set of characteristics that an entity has in some context, such as responsibilities it may have, action (behaviors) it may execute, or pieces of knowledge that it is expected to have in that context, which are referenced to by a specific role name.

author: No author specified
content: Scope of Control​

author: No author specified
content: the extent of the area or subject matter that a party controls.

author: No author specified
content: Scope​

author: No author specified
content: the extent of the area or subject matter (which we use, e.g., to define pattern, concept, term and glossaries in, but it serves other purposes as well).

author: No author specified
content: Term (Scoped)​

author: No author specified
content: a term, the meaning of which is determined by the definition to which it refers in a specific scope/context.

author: No author specified
content: Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI)​

author: No author specified
content: self-Sovereign Identity (SSI) is a term that has many different interpretations, and that we use to refer to concepts/ideas, architectures, processes and technologies that aim to support (autonomous) parties as they negotiate and execute electronic transaction with one another.

author: No author specified
content: Self-Sovereignty​

author: No author specified
content: the characteristic of every party that it is autonomous in managing and operating its owns knowledge, particularly in making decisions and deciding how to decide.

author: No author specified
content: Semantic Unit​

author: No author specified
content: a basic building block of meaning or representation that exists within the 'mind' of a party (i.e., in its knowledge).

author: No author specified
content: Semantics​

author: No author specified
content: a mapping between the (tangible/textual) term and (intangible) ideas/concept - their meaning.

author: No author specified
content: Semantics Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that can be used to explain the relations between the (intangible) concepts (and other semantic units) that are part of a party's knowledge, and how they are (tangibly) represented by terms, attributes, etc.

author: No author specified
content: SSI Agent​

author: No author specified
content: a digital agent that provides one or more of the ssi functionalities (issuer, holder, verifier, wallet) to its principal.

author: No author specified
content: SSI Assurance Community (SSI-AC)​

author: No author specified
content: a community that supports its members as they seek to increase their confidence in the SSI infrastructure and/or (specific) qualifications of the data exchanged through that infrastructure.

author: No author specified
content: SSI Infrastructure​

author: No author specified
content: the technological components that are (envisaged to be) all over the world for the purpose of providing, requesting and obtaining qualified data, for the purpose of negotiating and/or executing electronic transaction.

author: No author specified
content: SSI (Self-Sovereign Identity)​

author: No author specified
content: sSI (Self-Sovereign Identity) is a term that has many different interpretations, and that we use to refer to concepts/ideas, architectures, processes and technologies that aim to support (autonomous) parties as they negotiate and execute electronic transaction with one another.

author: No author specified
content: Subject​

author: No author specified
content: the (single) entity to which a given set of coherent data relates/pertains. Examples of such sets include attributes, Claims/Assertions, files/dossiers, (verifiable) credentials, (partial) identities, employment contracts, etc.

author: No author specified
content: Tag​

author: No author specified
content: an alphanumeric string that is used to identify scope (so called 'scopetags'), group term (so called 'grouptags'), or identify a specific version of a terminology (so called 'versiontags') from within a specific scope.

author: No author specified
content: Term​

author: No author specified
content: a word or phrase (i.e.: text) that is used in at least one scope/context to represent a specific concept.

author: No author specified
content: Terminology Process​

author: No author specified
content: a method for recognizing misunderstandings as such, and creating or maintaining definition that resolve them.

author: No author specified
content: Terminology​

author: No author specified
content: the set of term that are used within a single scope to refer to a single definition, enabling parties to reason and communicate ideas they have about one or more specific topics.

author: No author specified
content: Terminology Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that one can use to explain one's thinking about a certain topic, as well as to devise/design a way of thinking that members of a community can use to align their thinking as they pursue that community's objectives.

author: No author specified
content: Terms Community​

author: No author specified
content: a community that maintains a terminology for the purpose of avoiding misunderstandings between its members as they collaborate.

author: No author specified
content: Transaction Agreement​

author: No author specified
content: the set of rules that specify the rights (expectation) and duties (obligation) of participant towards one another in the context of a specific business transaction.

author: No author specified
content: Transaction Form​

author: No author specified
content: the specification of the set of data that this party needs to (a) commit to a (proposed) business transaction of that kind, (b) fulfill its duties/obligation and (c) escalate if necessary.

author: No author specified
content: Transaction Id​

author: No author specified
content: character string that this participant uses to identify, and refer to, that business transaction.

author: No author specified
content: Transaction Proposal​

author: No author specified
content: a transaction agreement that is 'in-the-making' (ranging from an empty document to a document that would be a transaction agreement if it were signed by all participant).

author: No author specified
content: Transaction Request​

author: No author specified
content: a message, send by a requesting party to a providing party, that initiates the negotiation of a new transaction agreement between these parties for the provisioning of a specific product or service.

author: No author specified
content: Transaction​

author: No author specified
content: the exchange of goods, services, funds, or data between some parties (called participant of the transaction).

author: No author specified
content: Trust level​

author: No author specified
content: the (subjective) degree of belief or confidence that a party has in X (someone, something, ...).

author: No author specified
content: Trust​

author: No author specified
content: the (un)conscious decision by a party to believe that X is in fact the case.

author: No author specified
content: Trust Pattern​

author: No author specified
content: a set of concepts and other semantic units that can be used to explain how the concept of trust relates to parties that do the trusting, and other concepts that express what it is that these parties trust.

author: No author specified
content: Validate​

author: No author specified
content: the act, by or on behalf of a party, of determining whether or not that data is valid to be used for some specific purpose(s) of that party.

author: No author specified
content: Validator Policy​

author: No author specified
content: a digital policy that enables an operational validator component to function in accordance with the objective of its principal.

author: No author specified
content: Validator​

author: No author specified
content: a component that implements the capability to determine whether or not (verified) data is valid to be used for some specific purpose(s).

author: No author specified
content: Verifier Policy​

author: No author specified
content: a digital policy that enables an operational verifier component to function in accordance with the objective of its principal.

author: No author specified
content: Verifier​

author: No author specified
content: a component that implements the capability to request peer agents to present (provide) data from credentials (of a specified kind, issued by specified parties), and to verify such responses (check structure, signatures, dates), according to its principal's verifier policy.

author: No author specified
content: Verify​

author: No author specified
content: the act, by or on behalf of a party, of determining whether that data is authentic (i.e. originates from the party that authored it), timely (i.e. has not expired), and conforms to other specifications that apply to its structure.

author: No author specified
content: Vocabulary​

author: No author specified
content: the sum or stock of words employed by a language, group, individual, or work or in a field of knowledge.

author: No author specified
content: Wallet Policy​

author: No author specified
content: a digital policy that enables an operational wallet component to function in accordance with the objective of its principal.

author: No author specified
content: Wallet​

author: No author specified
content: a component that implements the capability to securely store data as requested by colleague agents, and to provide stored data to colleague agents or peer agents, all in compliance with the rules of its principal's wallet policy.

author: No author specified
content: eSSIF-Lab World Model​

author: No author specified
content: the set of concept, relations between them (pattern), and Principles (that are the starting point for eSSIF-Lab's thinking).

author: No author specified
content: eSSIF-Lab Objectives

author: No author specified
content: Purpose​

author: No author specified
content: The purpose of the eSSIF-Lab is to specify, develop and validate technological and non-technological (e.g. governance) means that support people, businesses and governments to think about, design, adapt, and operate their (information) processes such that they can negotiate and conduct business transactions with one another using the electronic support provided by the various SSI technologies.

author: No author specified
content: Objectives​

author: No author specified
content: For this purpose, eSSIF-Lab has set the following objectives to realize:

author: No author specified
content: Empower European and other citizens​

author: No author specified
content: The results of the work done in eSSIF-Lab should contribute to provide individuals with means that help them to electronically negotiate and conduct transactions in the widest sense of the word. Such means make it easier for them to participate in transactions, both on the Internet and in physical life.

author: No author specified
content: Criteria to determine that the objective is fulfilled include:

author: No author specified
content: logins (username, password) are eliminated.

author: No author specified
content: people can fill in forms (e.g. for applying for products or services) fast, and easily.

author: No author specified
content: people are protected from transacting with malicious parties.

author: No author specified
content: people find the provided means helpful as they seek to exercise their rights under privacy acts such as the GDPR.

author: No author specified
content: Empower European and other organizations and governments​

author: No author specified
content: The results of the work done in eSSIF-Lab should contribute to provide organizations (e.g. enterprises, governments) with means that help them to electronically negotiate and conduct transactions in the widest sense of the word. Such means make it easier for them to participate in transactions, both on the Internet and in physical life.
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Empower European and other organizations and governments​

author: No author specified
content: Criteria to determine that the objective is fulfilled include:
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Empower European and other organizations and governments​

author: No author specified
content: the costs of validating the data that organizations need to determine whether or not to commit to a transaction, is significantly reduced.

author: No author specified
content: the lead time of (administrative) processes, such as applying for a product or service, is significantly reduced.

author: No author specified
content: the efforts of satisfying the requirements regarding their duties under privacy acts such as the GDPR, and showing compliance, is significantly reduced.

author: No author specified
content: new business ecosystem paradigms materialize, with thousands of new jobs, many new job categories and new business opportunities for existing and new European companies.

author: No author specified
content: Design and Develop an SSI Infrastructure​

author: No author specified
content: The SSI-Infrastructure* is the 'hard' (IT) Infrastructure that we need to generically support parties (enterprises, governments, individuals, ...). The result of the work done in eSSIF-Lab should contribute to the creation (and maintenance) of components that are fitting in an SSI Infrastructure.
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Design and Develop an SSI Infrastructure​

author: No author specified
content: Criteria to determine that the objective is fulfilled include:
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Design and Develop an SSI Infrastructure​

author: No author specified
content: 'horizontal interoperability', i.e. the components that are part of the SSI Infrastructure work (interoperate) with other components of that infrastructure;

author: No author specified
content: 'vertical interoperability', i.e. the components that are part of the SSI Infrastructure can be used very easily by application software and other non-infrastructural (technical) components,

author: No author specified
content: 'documentation interoperability', i.e. ensuring/facilitating that documentation of the infrastructural (and perhaps other) components can use a single, well-defined terminology that is based on validated mental models.

author: No author specified
content: Design and Develop SSI Assurance Communities​

author: No author specified
content: SSI Assurance Communities (ACs) is the 'soft' Infrastructure that complements the (hard) SSI Infrastructure. The result of the work done in eSSIF-Lab should contribute to the creation (and maintenance) of communities that provide governance, management and optionally technical components and or services that facilitate the adoption of SSI and transformation of its members internal organization, processes and IT, such that it can start, or continue to use the SSI infrastructure and reap its projected benefits.
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Design and Develop SSI Assurance Communities​

author: No author specified
content: Criteria to determine that the objective is fulfilled include:
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Design and Develop SSI Assurance Communities​

author: No author specified
content: the number of SSI Assurance Communities is non-zero, and growing;

author: No author specified
content: processes, procedures and other mechanisms for the governance of SSI-related technology, data and administrative processes have become available and are being used;'

author: No author specified
content: (IT) tooling that supports SSI-ACs become available, such as credential catalogues, accreditation schemes that apply to the issuing of certain kinds of credentials, etc.

author: No author specified
content: Overview of eSSIF-Lab Mental Models

author: No author specified
content:
Within eSSIF-Lab, we maintain a set of mental models, which we also call patterns, which are descriptions, both casual and formal, of sets of concepts (ideas), relations between them, and constraints, that together form a coherent and consistent 'viewpoint', or 'way of thinking' about a certain topic.1  They have been crafted so that they may serve as a basis for architecting, designing, and implementing IT components and their governance processes.
We stress that while they represent a specific way of looking at things, there are many more, and in the end, all that matters is the extent in which they are successfully used.

author: No author specified
imgMeta: Overview of eSSIF-Lab Mental Models Within eSSIF-Lab, we maintain a set of mental models, which we also call patterns, which are descriptions, both casual and formal, of sets of concepts (ideas), relations between them, and constraints, that together form a coherent and consistent 'viewpoint', or 'way of thinking' about a certain topic.1 They have been crafted so that they may serve as a basis for architecting, designing, and implementing IT components and their governance processes. We stress that while they represent a specific way of looking at things, there are many more, and in the end, all that matters is the extent in which they are successfully used.  One might think that everyone has their own mental models, and uses them to make decisions, make sense of the world, etc. Any mental mo

author: No author specified
content: One might think that everyone has their own mental models, and uses them to make decisions, make sense of the world, etc. Any mental model that helps a person cope in the world is ok. For example, the ancient Greeks had a mental model that said the earth is at the center of the universe, and the sun and planets somehow revolve around it. Further development of the model allowed them to compute planetary positions, which played a role in fortune telling and therefore was considered important. Doing this is very complex. In the 16th century, Copernic revolutionized science by stating that the sun, rather than the earth, was at the centre of the universe. It wasn't like he changed the universe itself - he only changed the frame of reference, the perspective, i.e. the mental model that he used to look at, reason with, and explain the universe. Copernic showed in a dramatic way that changing one's perspective can have very profound consequences.

author: No author specified
content: Most changes of perspective do not have such large effects. If he had proposed to put Jupiter at the centre of the universe, or the moon, he would have ended up with different mental models, but the complexity of computing planetary positions would have remained equally complex. Changing ones point of reference to the sun, however, made things much easier: planetary trajectories could now be seen as simple ellipses, with the sun positioned at one of the focal points.

author: No author specified
content: 'Mental modeling' is the art (yes, as far as we know it's not a science) of creating models that make things easy. It's like 'searching for the sun'. We have been mental modeling artisans for several years now, and we have come up with models that are stable, in the sense that we have been using them and have found them to be quite helpful. Also, we have slightly less mature models, that need to be used in practice to see how well they work. And we have some models that we are in the process of describing, which we present here as ongoing work.

author: No author specified
content: Stable Models​

author: No author specified
content: These are models that are mature (stable). They have been applied during several years in various circumstances, and have shown to be valid (when consistently and consequently applied). Therefore, they are proposed for widespread use (and further evaluation/validation).

author: No author specified
content: Parties, Actors and Actions​

author: No author specified
content: The Parties, Actors and Actions pattern captures the foundational concepts and relations that we need for thinking about how things get done. It answers questions such as: 'Who/what does things?', 'How are their actions being guided/controlled?', 'Who controls whom/what?', 'Who/what may be held accountable?'.

author: No author specified
content: Jurisdictions​

author: No author specified
content: The Jurisdictions pattern captures the concepts and relations that explain what a generic jurisdiction consists of, and relates it to parties and legal entities.

author: No author specified
content: Guardianships​

author: No author specified
content: The Guardianship pattern captures the concepts and relations that explain what a generic guardianship consists of, and how it relates to guardians, dependents, jurisdictions, etc.

author: No author specified
content: Models under review​

author: No author specified
content: These are models that we think go a long way to being mature, but may contain flaws we haven't detected yet.

author: No author specified
content: Terminology​

author: No author specified
content: The eSSIF-Lab Terminology Pattern will describe the relations between terminology artifacts such as 'concept', 'term', 'pattern', 'mental model', 'glossary' etc.

author: No author specified
content: Identity​

author: No author specified
content: The eSSIF-Lab Identity Pattern

author: No author specified
content: discusses difficulties that exist with the various/numerous meanings of the term 'identity',

author: No author specified
content: postulates a definition for identity that relates to what a person or another entity actually is,

author: No author specified
content: shows that it is comprised of partial identities that are the actual artifacts we need to focus on in SSI contexts, and

author: No author specified
content: shows how this relates to (attributes in) credentials.

author: No author specified
content: Identification​

author: No author specified
content: The eSSIF-Lab Identity Pattern will describe the concepts and relations that help to explain the mechanisms that a party uses to identify entities, and mechanisms for communicating with another party such that both parties can identify an entity and know whether or not they identify the same entity.

author: No author specified
content: Identifiers​

author: No author specified
content: The eSSIF-Lab Identity Pattern will describe the conceptual nature of identifiers. Note that identifiers are very different from identities.

author: No author specified
content: Party Representation​

author: No author specified
content: The Party Representation pattern will capture the foundational concepts and relations that we need for thinking about how parties can represent one another in various circumstances, and answering questions such as 'in what ways can parties be represented?', 'what kind(s) of entities can represent parties', 'how can we deal with representation constraints, i.e. provide guarantees that the represented party isn't completely at the mercy of the one representing it?'.

author: No author specified
content: Governance and Management​

author: No author specified
content: The Governance and Management Pattern will explain how parties organize that their objectives are realized, either by doing the associated work themselves, or by arranging for other parties to do that. The contribution of this pattern is to show how this is done, based on the idea that every objective has a single party that owns the objective.

author: No author specified
content: Decentralized Governance, Risk Management and Compliance (GRC)​

author: No author specified
content: The Decentralized GRC pattern will describe how parties can set objectives, and pursue them to be successful. The latter means that the party must be capable of assessing and managing the risks associated with not realizing them. In a decentralized world, this means that it needs to depend on other parties, that may or may not be too reliable. Also, it means that the party must be able to set and realize objectives to satisfy requirements of other parties (compliance).
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Decentralized Governance, Risk Management and Compliance (GRC)​

author: No author specified
content: Envisaged Models​

author: No author specified
content: These are placeholders for models that we think we could document, but haven't come around to doing.

author: No author specified
content: eSSIF-Lab World Model​

author: No author specified
content: The eSSIF-Lab World Model will describe the basic concepts, relations between them (patterns), and principles (that are the starting point for eSSIF-Lab's thinking) that eSSIF-Lab proposes as a basis for designing, implementing and deploying architectures, processes and technologies that aim to support (autonomous, self-sovereign) parties as they negotiate and execute electronic transactions with one another.

author: No author specified
content: Trust​

author: No author specified
content: The eSSIF-Lab Trust Pattern will describe the conceptual nature of trust - limited to SSI contexts

author: No author specified
content: Mandates, Delegation and Hiring​

author: No author specified
content: The envisaged Mandates, Delegation and Hiring pattern will capture the ideas behind Mandating, Delegating, Hiring and their relations. It will extend the Parties, Actors and Actions pattern with concepts that describe how the ownership and works for relations between parties and actors are to be (de)populated, and how to determine for party the actor is working as it executes an action.

author: No author specified
content: works for

author: No author specified
content: Duties and Rights​

author: No author specified
content: The Duties and Rights pattern will describe the relations between jurisdictions, legal entities and the duties and rights they have within them. This pattern will be based on the theory of Hohfeld.

author: No author specified
content: Decision Making​

author: No author specified
content: The Decision Making pattern will describe how parties would, could, or should reason in order to reach good conclusions and make good decisions. This can be used as a basis for understanding the information needs of  parties as they need to decide e.g. whether or not to commit to a transaction proposal, or whether or not data is valid for some purpose. The pattern is based on Toulmin's model for reasoning (of which a pragmatical text can be found here).

author: No author specified
content: Semantics​

author: No author specified
content: The Semantics pattern describes the relations between (intangible) concepts that are part of a Party's Knowledge, and how they are (tangibly) represented. This mapping between what is known (yet is intangible) and what can actually be obtained, stored, processed and transmitted (because it is tangible) is otherwise known as a semantics.

author: No author specified
content: Footnotes​

author: No author specified
content: Others also call this 'ontologies'. Here are some lecture notes on the systematic constructions of an ontology by Th. P. van der Weide (2007). ↩

author: No author specified
content: Others also call this 'ontologies'. Here are some lecture notes on the systematic constructions of an ontology by Th. P. van der Weide (2007). ↩

author: No author specified
content: eSSIF-Lab Principles

author: No author specified
content: The dialogue about what Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI) really is, that was started in the blog "The Path to Self-Sovereign Identity" by Christopher Allen in 2016, has not resulted in a consensus today. While some see the ten principles of SSI that Allen proposed as the definition of SSI, they have been formulated as "a departure point to provoke a discussion about what's truly important".

author: No author specified
content: From the perspective of eSSIF-Lab this "what's truly important" relates to electronically supporting parties as they negotiate and execute (business) transactions. From this perspective, it seems reasonable to have the term SSI refer to all concepts/ideas, architectures, processes and technologies that aim to support (autonomous) parties as they negotiate and execute electronic (business) transactions with one another.

author: No author specified
content: We are aware that others have different ideas about what SSI is. That's ok - it is consistent with our ideas that others are also autonomous, self-sovereign parties. However, it does suggest that it may be worthwhile to postulate the principles that eSSIF-Lab uses to underpin its vision, framework, etc.

author: No author specified
content: The purpose of this section is to provide the eSSIF-Lab principles on which our eSSIF-Lab Framework is based. They are the 'axioms' we use for our reasoning, making choices (e.g. for terminology), etc. We think that making them explicit helps readers to decide whether or not they want to adopt (parts of) the framework.

author: No author specified
content: Freedom of Information (Knowledge)​

author: No author specified
