author: No author specified
content: Ecosystem

author: No author specified
content: Short Description​

author: No author specified
content: An Ecosystem is a set of at least two (autonomous) parties (the members of the ecosystem) whose individual work complements that of other members, and is of benefit to the set as a whole.

author: No author specified
content: An ecosystem is distinct from a community in the sense that it is not a party in its own right (which a community is): it does not have and pursue objectives, nor does it manage a knowledge. A community is considered a specialization of the more generic 'ecosystem' concept.

author: No author specified
content: Purpose​

author: No author specified
content: The ability to distinguish between an ecosystem and a non-ecosystem enables one to focus on its members, e.g. to learn how they get to complement each other's work without this being explicitly organized, to organize ways in which such cooperation can be made sustainable without explicitly needing to organize or control that, etc. Being part of an ecosystem allows individual parties to focus on their strengths and to rely on others to provide for its needs, and being appreciated for the value of its own work as it provides for the needs of others, without the need of explicitly organizing all this.

author: No author specified
content: Criterion​

author: No author specified
content: An Ecosystem is a set of at least two (autonomous) parties whose individual work complements that of others, and is of benefit to the set as a whole.

author: No author specified
content: Employee

author: No author specified
content: Additional content is needed here.

author: No author specified
content: Short Description​

author: No author specified
content: An Employee (of a Party) is a (human or non-human (digital)) actor for whom/which it is realistic that it might execute actions on behalf of a party (called the employer of that actor). Typically, (an actor) becoming an employee of a party is the result of its (successful) onboarding by that party.

author: No author specified
content: In practice, the term 'employee' often implies a person that is onboarded by an organization. In our way of thinking, however, this meaning is too limited. Here are some examples of actors that also qualify in the role of employee:

author: No author specified
content: a person (as an actor) is always an employee of itself (as a party);

author: No author specified
content: a technical (digital) component (actor) that is controlled by a party (implying that it is also onboarded), also qualifies as an employee of that party.

author: No author specified
content: any actor (human or non-human) that is mandated for executing certain actions on behalf of some party, qualifies as an employee of that party.

author: No author specified
content: The Parties, Actors and Actions pattern provides an overview of how this concept fits in with related concepts.

author: No author specified
content: Purpose​

author: No author specified
content: Criteria​

author: No author specified
content: Employer

author: No author specified
content: Additional content is needed here.

author: No author specified
content: Short Description​

author: No author specified
content: An Employer (of an Actor) is a party on whose behalf a (human or non-human) actor (called an employee of that party) might execute actions. Typically, that would be the result of the the party having (successfully) onboarded the actor.

author: No author specified
content: In practice, the term 'employer' often implies a party that has onboarded one or more people. In our way of thinking, however, this meaning is too limited. Here are some examples of parties that also qualify in the role of employer:

author: No author specified
content: a person (as an party) is always an employer of itself (as a actor);

author: No author specified
content: a party that controls a technical component (an actor, that is then also onboarded), also qualifies as an employer of that component.

author: No author specified
content: a party that has mandated an actor (human or non-human) for executing certain actions on its behalf, qualifies as an employer of that actor.

author: No author specified
content: The Parties, Actors and Actions pattern provides an overview of how this concept fits in with related concepts.

author: No author specified
content: Purpose​

author: No author specified
content: Criteria​

author: No author specified
content: Employment Contract

author: No author specified
content: Short Description​

author: No author specified
content: An employment contract is an agreement/contract between two parties, one of which controls a set of actors (the subjects of the contract), that states (or refers to) all (sets of) rights and duties under which these actors can and/or must work for the other party. This includes rights and duties of both parties, as well as rights and duties of the actor(s) that are the subject of the contract.

author: No author specified
content: Rights and duties pertain to e.g., (groups of) actions that the actor(s) are expected (not) to execute, which policies they would be expected (not) to follow, the conditions under which the actor(s) should work (e.g. times, locations, ...), the provisioning of any means needed to perform, etc. And of course, the contract would also state how the party that controls these actors will be remunerated.

author: No author specified
content: The creation of employment contracts are part of what is called onboarding (of an actor, that is a subject of the employment contract, by the party that wants to deploy the actor as one of its agents. More details are given in the party actor action pattern.

author: No author specified
content: Examples​

author: No author specified
content: An individual may sign a labor contract, that allows it to work for an organization in a designated function, for which it receives a salary. The individual and the organization, which both qualify as parties, sign the contract, which states the conditions under which the individual (as an actor) works for the organization. The function represents the kinds of actions that it would be executing, and there would be a section on working hours/locations, etc.

author: No author specified
content: An individual that is an employee for an employment agency would have a similar contract with that agency that provides it with a measure of control over the individual that enables it to negotiate employment contracts with other organizations. The actor (subject) of such an employment contract is the individual, and the other organization and the employment agency are the parties that sign the employment contract.

author: No author specified
content: Accountants that are employed by an accountancy firm have a contract with the accountancy firm stating the conditions (rights and duties of either) under which they work for that firm. When the firm receives an order from some organization for an accountancy job, it can then assign the accountant to that job. However, while this accountant may work at the premises of the organization, the latter will not have onboarded

author: No author specified
content: The Parties, Actors and Actions pattern describes how this concept fits in with related concepts.

author: No author specified
content: Purpose​

author: No author specified
content: The purpose of having employment contracts is to ensure that expectations that parties have towards one another, and towards actors that work for them, are matched by corresponding obligations of the reciprocal party (for details on matching, see the section Matching of the Governance and Management pattern). An employment contract that is made explicit, and electronically available, may also serve as part of the chain of assets that are needed to determine the right (or duty) of an actor to execute an action on behalf of some party.

author: No author specified
content: Criteria​

author: No author specified
content: An employment contract is an agreement/contract

author: No author specified
content: between two parties, one of which controls a set of actors (the subjects of the contract);

author: No author specified
content: that states (or refers to) all (sets of) rights and duties under which these actors can and/or must work for the other party;

author: No author specified
content: that includes rights and duties of both parties, as well as rights and duties of the actor(s) that are the subject of the contract.

author: No author specified
content: Entity

author: No author specified
content: Short Description​

author: No author specified
content: Whenever you know that somethings exists, be it another person, yourself, some computer, an extinct animal, a thought, an idea, a JSON-object, ..., anything you can think of, is what the term Entity refers to.

author: No author specified
content: The terminology pattern provides an overview of how this concept fits in with related concepts.

author: No author specified
content: Purpose​

author: No author specified
content: This term enables us to refer to anything, or to postulate the existence of something, without further specifying what it is, or how it might be named.

author: No author specified
content: Criterion​

author: No author specified
content: Something, anything, that some party knows to exist

author: No author specified
content: Related Concepts​

author: No author specified
content: a legal entity is an entity that is known by (i.e. registered in the knowledge of) a party that operates the legal system of a jurisdiction. (Details are in the Jurisdictions pattern)

author: No author specified
content: Scope: essifLabTerminology

author: No author specified
content: Governance​

author: No author specified
content: The eSSIF-Lab project governs the terminology within this scope, according to the procedures mentioned in the eSSIF-Lab Framework.

author: No author specified
content: Objectives/Issues​

author: No author specified
content: Contributors to and users of eSSIF-Lab come from various backgrounds. Their culture may not be Western. English may not be their native tongue. They may be experts in non-technological topics. Working with one another presumes a setting where participants have some level of shared understanding. Often, sharing one's understanding at a superficial level suffices. Other situations require that underlying concepts are shared in a more in-depth fashion. It's like cars: people buying, selling, or driving cars do not need in-depth shared knowledge about cars, whereas (maintenance or construction) engineers or liability lawyers need to share a deeper knowledge of how cars do (or do not) work.

author: No author specified
content: We expect to see situations of "language confusion", i.e. in which people use words or phrases, the intension (not: intention) of which differs from the interpretation of some listeners/readers. Sometimes a casual glance at a dictionary or glossary is the solution. In other cases, deeper understanding matters, e.g. in when drafting specifications or contracts. Then we need more than a set of definitions.

author: No author specified
content: The Concepts and Terminology part of eSSIF-Lab aims helps eSSIF-Lab community participants understand one another at whatever level of precision they need.

author: No author specified
content: Expectation

author: No author specified
content: Short Description​

author: No author specified
content: An Expectation is an objective that is owned by a party for having a specific (set of) result(s) produced, where the actual production thereof is (going to be) outsourced to another party.

author: No author specified
content: In the figure below, objectives Obj-1b.1 and Obj-1b.3 are expectations of Red towards Blue and Green respectively. Also, objective Obj-Yel-m is an expectation of Yellow towards Red.

author: No author specified
content: Figure 1: Chained Objectives - results produced in one objective are consumed in another

author: No author specified
imgMeta: of is (going to be) outsourced to another party. In the figure below, objectives Obj-1b.1 and Obj-1b.3 are expectations of Red towards Blue and Green respectively. Also, objective Obj-Yel-m is an expectation of Yellow towards Red.  Figure 1: Chained Objectives - results produced in one objective are consumed in another The figure shows four parties (Red, Yellow, Blue and Green) and their associated scopes of control. Within these, they owns the objectives (the figure shows 6 objectives owned by red and one for each of the other parties). The figure in the top right hand corner of the rectangle that represents an objective, is the party that the owner of the objective expects to produce the objective's results. It is not necessary that such a party is known all the time (e.g. objective Obj-1a). The arrows indicate that results produced to realize a certain objective (at the source of the arrow) are (to be) used to produce the results of another objective (at the end/tip of the arrow).

content: The figure shows four parties (Red, Yellow, Blue and Green) and their associated scopes of control. Within these, they owns the objectives (the figure shows 6 objectives owned by red and one for each of the other parties). The figure in the top right hand corner of the rectangle that represents an objective, is the party that the owner of the objective expects to produce the objective's results. It is not necessary that such a party is known all the time (e.g. objective Obj-1a).

author: No author specified

author: No author specified
content: For more information, e.g. about how different parties interact in their roles of producer and consumer, we refer you to the Governance and Management pattern.

author: No author specified
content: Framework (Conceptual)

author: No author specified
content: Short Description​

author: No author specified
content: A (conceptual) framework is set of assumptions, concepts, values, and practices that constitutes a way of viewing reality. The eSSIF-Lab framework is such a conceptual framework.

author: No author specified
content: Outside of eSSIF-Lab, the term 'framework' is also used in meanings such as defined by the American Heritage Dictionary:

author: No author specified
content: A structure for supporting or enclosing something else, especially a skeletal support used as the basis for something being constructed.

author: No author specified
content: An external work platform; a scaffold.

author: No author specified
content: A fundamental structure, as for a written work.

author: No author specified
content: A set of assumptions, concepts, values, and practices that constitutes a way of viewing reality.

author: No author specified
content: Glossary

author: No author specified
content: Short Description​

author: No author specified
content: A glossary is an alphabetically sorted list of terms with explanations, usually aimed to help people understand texts around a certain (set of) topic(s) in (at least) one context. A glossary may also be created for the purpose of being included in other glossaries (as a construction aid to such glossaries), or for still other purposes.

author: No author specified
content: The terminology pattern provides an overview of how this concept fits in with related concepts.

author: No author specified
content: Purpose​

author: No author specified
content: A glossary may serve various purposes, the most important one of which would be to help people understand texts around a certain (set of) topic(s) in some context(s).

author: No author specified
content: Criteria​

author: No author specified
content: A glossary is an alphabetical list of words or phrases with (short) explanations, that exists for the purpose of helping people to get a first understanding of the meaning of each word in the scope/context for which the glossary is created.

author: No author specified
content: Examples​

author: No author specified
content: Examples include the eSSIF-Lab Glossary, the Sovrin Glossary, the Glossary of Internet Terms, and glossaries for Legal Terms, e.g. of the US, Singapore, the UK.

author: No author specified
content: Related Concepts​

author: No author specified
content: Dictionary - this is more extensive; it usually includes multiple meanings of words, and may include additional information e.g. on pronunciation, etymology, usage, example sentences,synonyms, etc. See askdifference.com

author: No author specified
content: Vocabulary - this is a set of familiar words witin a particular/persons's language or field of expertise. A Dictionary can provide the various meanings of each such words.

author: No author specified
content: Notes​

author: No author specified
content: The eSSIF-Lab Glossary contains the words that are defined within the scope of the eSSIF-Lab framework.

author: No author specified
content: Governance

author: No author specified
content: Short Description​

author: No author specified
content: Governance is the act or process of governing or overseeing the realization of (the results associated with) a set of objectives that are owned by a single party, in order to ensure these results will be fit for the purposes that this party intends to use them for. This act or process is conducted by (agents that do so on behalf of) this party.

author: No author specified
content: Governance is about planning the budgets and other resources necessary  to (obtain and to) actually use the results. This includes the specification of timelines (deadlines) for the results to become available, as well as the properties and other characteristics (security, quality, sustainability, etc.) that results must have in order to make them effective ('fit for purpose' - fit to be used as intended by the consuming party). In order to keep tabs on the fitness of the results for the intended purposes, effectiveness indicators may be developed, i.e. gauges that measure how 'fit' the results are to be used/consumed for the intended purposes, are also part of this governance.

author: No author specified
content: The governance of a set of objectives can be implemented as a control process where each of these objectives serves as a control objective in that process.

author: No author specified
content: We make a clear distinction between governance and management, which is explained in the governance and management pattern.

author: No author specified
content: Purpose​

author: No author specified
content: The purpose for a party of having a governance process is that it enables him to oversee the realization of (the results associated with) various subset of its objectives, and to change the specification of the associated results, to select the appropriate party for actually realizing these results, etc.

author: No author specified
content: Governor

author: No author specified
content: This page is a first draft and may need revision.

author: No author specified
content: Short Description​

author: No author specified
content: A Governor is a role that a party (better: employee of a specific party) performs asit governs a (coherent) set of expectations of that party, within a particular focus.

author: No author specified
content: Within organizations, these [expectations] are typically owned by an organizational unit (which iself qualifies as a party), e.g. a board of directors or a department, and directed at other organizational units. These expectations may link arbitrary organizational units, not just up and down the organizational hierarchy, but also to units at the same organization level, or at other (arbitrary) locations in the hierarchy.

author: No author specified
content: Every focus area, such as finance, security, (regulatory) compliance, etc. typically has its own governance process. This allows for the efficient assignment of governors to the governance process of a specific focal area, e.g. to employees that have relevant "domain knowledge" and background for running such processes.

author: No author specified
content: Within the context of SSI, knowledge- and information governance is a particular topic, and part of the results that are to be created and maintained are the various policies that provide guidance to employees for executing (both design-time and runtime) actions that have some relation with credentials.

author: No author specified
content: We will use the phrase "<topic> governor", where <topic> is a placeholder for a particular topic/domain, to refer to the governor that will govern the expectations of a single party that pertain to that particular topic/domain. For example: "knowledge governor" refers to the governor role that governs the expectations of a single party that pertain to that its knowledge. Similarly, we might use "quality governor", "security governor", etc.

author: No author specified
content: <topic>

author: No author specified
content: <topic>

author: No author specified
content: Purpose​

author: No author specified
content: In order to ensure that expectations (that a party has regarding acertain topic/domain) are being met, or revised if they cannot be met, it is necessary that the party can task employees that have relevant "domain knowledge" with running a governance-process for such expectations, and hence fulfill the role we have called governor

author: No author specified
content: Criteria​

author: No author specified
content: A Governor is a role that a party (better: employee of a specific party) performs asit governs a (coherent) set of expectations of that party, within a particular focus.

content: Guardian
